caption: FIG. 1. (a) Spread of film thickness over the position across the wafer table. The error bars correspond to 95% confidence intervals. The horizontal dashed line represents the mean value of 6.1 nm within + 50 mm from (0 , 0). (b) Distribution of root mean square (RMS) surface roughness over a 6-in. area. The horizontal dashed line represents the mean value of 0.82 nm within + 50 mm. (c) Exemplary 3 /C2 3 μ m 2 AFM image at position y ¼ /C0 37 : 5 mm with the mean value as zero level.
image_path: extracted/Knehr et al. - 2021 - Wafer-level uniformity of atomic-layer-deposited niobium nitride thin films for quantum devices/figures/figure-016.png
enhanced_caption: bridges are 1 μ m wide and 10 μ m long (see geometry in the inset of Fig. 3). This comparatively wide structure was chosen to separate film-inherent inhomogeneities from any adverse effect caused by the patterning process. To avoid current crowding and maximize the switching current, 31 the voltage leads to the microbridge are rounded off. The patterns were defined by electron-beam lithography using 130 nm thick ma-N 2401 resist exposed to electrons of acceleration voltage 50 kV with a dose density of 495 μ C/cm 2 . After development, the written structures were transferred into the NbN layer by Ar-ion milling. FIG. 1. (a) Spread of film thickness over the position across the wafer table. The error bars correspond to 95% confidence intervals. The horizontal dashed line represents the mean value of 6.1 nm within + 50 mm from (0 , 0). (b) Distribution of root mean square (RMS) surface roughness over a 6-in. area. The horizontal dashed line represents the mean value of 0.82 nm within + 50 mm. (c) Exemplary 3 /C2 3 μ m 2 AFM image at position y ¼ /C0 37 : 5 mm with the mean value as zero level. Figure 1(a) shows the distribution of film thickness along the x - and y -axis of the examined area. Within a diameter of 4 in. ( x , y j j /C20 50 mm), there is a negligible variation in thickness around a mean value of 6.1 nm, equivalent to a film inhomogeneity of 3.6%. This corresponds to a growth rate of 0.469 Å/cycle, which is comparable to previously reported values. 25,26 Increasing the area to 6 in. leads to 9.1% inhomogeneity and a maximum thickness of 7 : 0 + 0 : 3 nm ( y ¼ /C0 75 mm).

vision_decision.reason: The image contains multiple panels with data plots, labeled (a), (b), and (c), which include scatter plots with axes, error bars, and a microscopy texture image. The presence of these elements is typical of scientific figures used to visualize data and results in research papers.

caption: FIG. 2. (a) SIMS: distribution of nitrogen and carbon counts relative to the respective niobium count at each position. Both relative curves are normalized, i.e., the absolute values of nitrogen and carbon cannot be compared. The dashed lines are guides to the eye. (b) XRD: 2 Θ -scan ( ω fixed at 0.4 /C14 ) of 20 nm thick ALD-NbN at three positions along the x -axis of the wafer. The two peaks around 35.5 /C14 and 41.5 /C14 correspond to orientations (111) and (200) of cubic δ -NbN, respectively.

caption: FIG. 3. Distribution of critical temperature (a), superconducting transition width (b), residual resistivity at 25 K (c), and switching current density at 4.2 K (d) over the sample ' s position on the wafer along the x - and y -axis. The measurements were conducted on microbridges of 1 μ m width and 6.1 nm thickness (see the scanning electron micrograph in the inset). The vertical dashed lines illustrate wafer edges of the denoted sizes.

caption: FIG. 4. (a) Distribution of the ratio of fitted current density j fit to theoretical depairing current density j dep at 0 K over the wafer area. (b) and (c) Exemplary j sw ( T )-curves at x ¼ /C0 47 : 8 mm (b) and x ¼ 0 : 7 mm (c). The dots represent j sw measured on bridges of 1 μ m width, the dashed curves denote fits by Eq. (1) to the measured data in the range of 0 : 8 , T = T c , 1 : 0 (indicated by the shaded area), and the solid line shows the depairing current density j dep, obtained from j dep (0) and temperature dependence in Eq. (1).
